h1Title: Ranula: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: A ranula is a mucous retention or extravasation cyst arising from the sublingual salivary gland, appearing as a translucent bluish swelling on the floor of the mouth. Simple ranulas remain confined to the oral floor; plunging (cervical) ranulas herniate through the mylohyoid muscle into the neck, presenting as a painless neck mass without an obvious intraoral component. Ranulas occur predominantly in children, adolescents, and young adults, with no strong sex predilection. They are caused by ductal obstruction or trauma, leading to pooling of mucin in the tissue. Surgical excision of the sublingual gland — not marsupialization alone — achieves the lowest recurrence rate, below 5%, compared with 60-90% recurrence after simple aspiration or marsupilization without gland removal.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 0.2 per 1,000 in the general population; cervical (plunging) ranulas account for approximately 10-15% of all ranula presentations
keyStats.demographics: Most common in the first three decades of life; no significant sex predilection; higher incidence in Asian and Pacific Islander populations in some epidemiologic series
keyStats.avgAge: Peak age 10-30 years; plunging ranulas present slightly later on average (peak 20-40 years)
keyStats.globalCases: True global incidence unknown; ranulas account for approximately 6-12% of all oral soft-tissue swelling presentations in pediatric oral surgery clinics

definition: A ranula (ICD-10: K11.6) is a pseudocyst or retention cyst of the floor of the mouth arising from the sublingual salivary gland or its excretory ducts. The term derives from the Latin rana (frog), describing the translucent, frog-belly-like appearance of the lesion. Pathophysiologically, ranulas result from either mucous extravasation (rupture of the ductal epithelium with pooling of saliva in surrounding connective tissue — true pseudocyst without epithelial lining) or mucous retention (ductal obstruction with distension of the duct and its lining — true cyst with epithelial lining). The sublingual gland produces purely mucous secretion, and its multiple small ducts (ducts of Rivinus) open directly onto the oral floor, making it the most common source. A simple oral ranula is confined to the sublingual space above the mylohyoid muscle. A plunging or cervical ranula occurs when the expanding mucous collection herniates through a natural dehiscence or acquired perforation in the mylohyoid muscle, dissecting into the submandibular or parapharyngeal space and presenting as a neck mass. Ranulas are managed by oral and maxillofacial surgeons; pediatric otolaryngologists may be involved for cervical ranulas in children.

typesClassification.description: Mucous cyst confined to the sublingual space, presenting as a visible translucent or bluish swelling on the floor of the mouth lateral to the frenulum. Does not extend into the neck. Majority of ranulas — 85-90% — are of this type. Usually presents in the first three decades of life.

Mucous extravasation that herniates through a mylohyoid dehiscence into the submandibular, parapharyngeal, or retropharyngeal space. Presents as a painless, non-tender, compressible neck mass. The intraoral component may be small or absent, making diagnosis less obvious without imaging.
Lesion with both an intraoral floor-of-mouth swelling and a continuous cervical component, representing herniation in progress. MRI demonstrates continuity through the mylohyoid gap.
Rare; present at birth, typically very large, causing airway compromise and feeding difficulty in neonates. Requires urgent surgical management, sometimes in utero or immediately postpartum.

primarySymptoms: A painless, slowly enlarging, translucent to bluish smooth swelling on the floor of the mouth lateral to the lingual frenulum — the classic presentation of a simple ranula, present in over 90% of cases.
A compressible, cystic consistency on palpation of the floor of the mouth swelling, fluctuating with fluid on digital examination.
Tongue elevation or displacement toward the contralateral side when the ranula enlarges sufficiently to fill the sublingual space.
Difficulty speaking (dysarthria), particularly with lingual consonants, when a large ranula restricts tongue mobility.
Dysphagia or difficulty swallowing when the ranula is large enough to elevate the tongue floor and reduce the pharyngeal volume.

Intermittent spontaneous deflation of the swelling followed by re-accumulation over days to weeks, as mucous leaks and reforms — a characteristic feature that helps distinguish ranulas from solid tumors.
A painless, compressible, non-tender neck mass in the submandibular region or at the angle of the jaw, representing a plunging ranula extending into the neck.
Transient visible swelling under the tongue after meals in early or intermittent ranulas, resolving spontaneously until the duct re-obstructs.
Bluish-purple discoloration of the floor-of-mouth swelling giving it the characteristic frog-belly appearance — most visible when the cyst is superficial and thinly covered by mucosa.
Rarely, difficulty breathing or drooling in large congenital or rapidly growing ranulas compressing the hypopharyngeal space.
earlyWarningSigns: A small, soft, intermittent swelling on the floor of the mouth that temporarily disappears after a meal and then re-forms — earliest sign of a developing ranula before it becomes persistent.
A subtle unilateral elevation of one side of the tongue floor in a young patient, noticed by a dentist during a routine examination.
A soft painless lump developing slowly over weeks in the submandibular area of a young adult without associated fever or tenderness — may indicate early plunging ranula.
A saliva taste followed by deflation of an intraoral floor swelling — spontaneous drainage of the cyst is a pathognomonic early feature.
emergencySigns: Rapid enlargement of a ranula causing tongue elevation, inability to swallow secretions, and stridor — airway emergency requiring immediate hospital evaluation.
Congenital ranula causing neonatal airway obstruction and inability to feed — requires emergent airway assessment and may need in utero fetal intervention or immediate postnatal surgery.
A rapidly growing neck mass displacing the trachea or causing dyspnea in a patient with known or suspected plunging ranula — indicates hemorrhage into the cyst or rare infection.
Infected ranula with overlying mucosa becoming fluctuant, bright red, and accompanied by systemic fever — requires urgent drainage to prevent deep neck space spread.

causes.description: Obstruction of the ducts of Rivinus (the multiple small excretory ducts of the sublingual gland) by salivary stones (sialolithiasis), mucous plugs, ductal stenosis, or scar tissue from prior trauma produces back-pressure dilatation and eventual ductal rupture with mucous extravasation into surrounding soft tissue, forming the pseudocyst characteristic of most ranulas.

causes.cause: Trauma to the sublingual gland region
causes.description: Direct trauma — from dental procedures, intubation, laceration, or chronic friction from dental appliances — disrupts the acinar or ductal epithelium of the sublingual gland, allowing mucinous secretion to accumulate extracellularly. This mechanism explains the higher incidence of ranulas in patients with bruxism, lower lip biting habits, or those who have undergone intraoral dental procedures.

No identifiable cause is found in approximately 30-40% of ranulas. Microscopic ductal disruptions from normal intraoral pressure changes or minor repetitive microtrauma during mastication may be sufficient to initiate extravasation in susceptible individuals.

In neonatal or congenital ranulas, a developmental defect in ductal formation — either atresia, stenosis, or abnormal connection between the sublingual gland and adjacent tissue — is thought to cause in-utero mucous accumulation and a lesion present at birth.

Incomplete removal of sublingual gland tissue during prior marsupialization or partial gland excision leaves residual secretory epithelium that can regenerate and re-form a mucous collection. This is the mechanism behind the high 60-90% recurrence rate of marsupialization alone and partial gland procedures.

riskFactors.description: Ranulas peak in the first three decades of life, possibly reflecting the high mucinous secretion rate of the sublingual gland in younger individuals and greater exposure to oral trauma from habits and dental treatment.

Dental extractions, local anesthetic injections, orthodontic procedures, and prosthetic appliances in the floor-of-mouth region can traumatize the sublingual gland ducts. Dentists should note persistent floor-of-mouth swelling post-operatively.

Stones in Wharton's duct or the ducts of Rivinus produce upstream pressure elevation and ductal dilatation that predispose to ranula formation. Submandibular stones are diagnosed by palpation and ultrasound.

riskFactors.factor: Parafunctional habits (lower lip biting, tongue thrusting)
riskFactors.description: Chronic intraoral pressure habits deliver repetitive microtrauma to the sublingual gland region. Individuals with bruxism or habitual floor-of-mouth trauma have a higher reported incidence of ranula formation.

riskFactors.factor: Prior marsupialization without sublingual gland removal
riskFactors.description: Marsupialization alone leaves the secretory source intact; recurrence is reported in 60-90% in series using this technique, compared with less than 5% when the sublingual gland is removed simultaneously.

Some epidemiologic series from Hawaii and Southeast Asia report higher ranula incidence in Asian and Pacific Islander populations, possibly related to sublingual gland anatomical variation or dietary factors. Evidence is observational.

affectedDemographics: Children and young adults aged 10-30 are the most commonly affected demographic, accounting for the majority of simple oral ranulas in most published series.
Plunging ranulas present slightly later, with peak incidence in the third and fourth decades, and may be missed for months due to the absence of a visible intraoral component.
Congenital ranulas are rare, representing fewer than 2% of cases, but are disproportionately important due to their potential to cause neonatal airway compromise.
No significant sex predilection is consistently demonstrated, though some series report a marginal female preponderance for simple ranulas.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of a simple ranula is usually clinical. A soft, translucent or bluish swelling on the floor of the mouth lateral to the frenulum in a child or young adult, with fluctuant consistency and the history of intermittent spontaneous deflation, is pathognomonic. Aspiration of viscous, clear-to-yellowish mucoid fluid with amylase and low protein content confirms the mucous nature. Differential diagnosis includes dermoid cyst (more midline, non-translucent, dough-like consistency), thyroglossal duct cyst (midline, elevates with tongue protrusion), oral mucocele (usually lower lip, smaller), and salivary gland neoplasm (firm, non-translucent). Imaging is mandatory for plunging ranulas, where the intraoral component may be subtle. MRI is the imaging modality of choice: it delineates the cyst's relationship to the mylohyoid muscle, demonstrates continuity between oral and cervical components, and characterizes the fluid signal (T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense mucous cyst). Ultrasound of the neck identifies the cervical component and guides aspiration for diagnostic cytology if needed. CT with contrast is useful when MRI is unavailable or when airway assessment is critical in a large plunging ranula. Cytological analysis of aspirated fluid (mucous, no epithelial cells in a pseudocyst) and biochemical testing (elevated salivary amylase) support the diagnosis. Biopsy of the cyst wall is usually not required but is performed when malignancy cannot be excluded.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms mucous content and distinguishes ranula from solid tumor; aspirated fluid is typically stringy, clear-to-amber, and highly viscous
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin needle is inserted into the swelling under the tongue under local anesthesia spray. The procedure takes 2-3 minutes. The viscous, thread-like mucous aspirated is characteristic. Mild pressure and bleeding may follow.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI of the floor of mouth and neck (T1, T2, gadolinium)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for plunging ranulas; delineates extent above and below the mylohyoid, identifies communication with cervical components, and excludes salivary gland malignancy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 20-40 minutes in the MRI scanner. Gadolinium contrast may be injected IV to characterize the cyst wall. No radiation. Results interpreted by a radiologist within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Ultrasound of the floor of mouth and neck
diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging for suspected plunging ranula when MRI is not immediately available; identifies a unilocular hypoechoic cervical cyst and its relationship to the submandibular gland
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 10-15 minute ultrasound with gel applied to the neck and floor of mouth. No radiation, no injection. Can guide aspiration for diagnostic cytology in the same session.

diagnosticTests.purpose: High salivary amylase content (>1,000 U/L) confirms salivary origin; low protein and no squamous cells differentiate from epidermoid cyst or lymphangioma fluid
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fluid collected during FNA is sent to the biochemistry laboratory. Results within 24-48 hours. High amylase strongly supports ranula diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Useful for large plunging ranulas to assess airway compromise, relationship to great vessels, and guide surgical planning when MRI is unavailable or patient cannot lie still
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 10-15 minute scan with IV contrast injection. Results within a few hours. Provides excellent anatomical roadmap for surgical planning.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Cytological examination of aspirated fluid cells; mucous without epithelial lining in a pseudocyst; excludes lymphoma or salivary gland malignancy when doubt remains
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin needle with a syringe is used to aspirate cells during FNA. Smears are prepared and examined by a cytopathologist. Results in 3-5 working days.

treatmentOverview: The definitive treatment for all ranula types — both simple oral and plunging cervical — is surgical removal of the ipsilateral sublingual gland. This approach addresses the root cause by eliminating the secretory source of the mucous extravasation, achieving recurrence rates below 5% in series of 20 years or more. Marsupialization alone (incising and suturing the cyst wall open to create a permanent drainage window) achieves short-term relief but leaves the gland intact; published recurrence rates are 60-90%, making it an inadequate standalone treatment. Marsupialization is currently used as a temporizing measure in children too young for general anesthesia or as an adjunct to immediate sublingual gland removal during the same procedure. Complete surgical technique for oral ranulas includes identification and preservation of Wharton's duct, lingual nerve, and hypoglossal nerve through careful intraoral dissection; the sublingual gland and cyst wall are removed en bloc. For plunging ranulas, the cervical component is typically reached through a submandibular or transcervical incision and drained, combined with intraoral sublingual gland excision in the same sitting. An alternative — injecting tetracycline, OK-432 (a streptococcal antigen preparation), or sodium tetradecyl sulfate into the cyst cavity to cause sclerosis — is reported as effective in select series (particularly for pediatric plunging ranulas) and avoids general anesthesia, but long-term data show higher recurrence and potential for cervical nerve injury compared with gland excision. AAOMS and BAOMS guidance favors sublingual gland excision as the definitive approach for all but the smallest or most recurrent simple ranulas in medically unfit patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Observation (for small, incidentally discovered, asymptomatic ranulas)
medicalTreatments.description: Small asymptomatic ranulas in adult patients may be observed with regular clinical examination every 3-6 months if the patient declines surgery and the lesion does not cause functional impairment. Spontaneous resolution has been reported in up to 20% of very small lesions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Spontaneous resolution in approximately 20% of small lesions; 80% require eventual surgical treatment due to growth or recurrence

medicalTreatments.name: OK-432 (Picibanil) intralesional injection
medicalTreatments.description: Lyophilized preparation of low-virulence Streptococcus pyogenes causing sterile inflammatory fibrosis within the cyst cavity. Injected under ultrasound guidance. Best evidence for plunging ranulas in pediatric patients where surgical risk is high.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete resolution in 70-80% of plunging ranulas in Japanese series (Roh 2006); recurrence higher than gland excision in direct comparisons

medicalTreatments.name: Sclerotherapy with sodium tetradecyl sulfate (STS)
medicalTreatments.description: Chemical sclerosant injected into the cyst cavity causing endothelial damage, inflammation, and fibrotic obliteration. Used primarily for recurrent or surgically inaccessible ranulas.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 60-70% success in published series but variable; not recommended as first-line by AAOMS guidelines

surgicalOptions.name: Sublingual gland excision with ranula removal (intraoral approach)
surgicalOptions.description: Definitive treatment under general anesthesia. The sublingual gland and attached cyst wall are removed through a floor-of-mouth incision, with careful identification and preservation of Wharton's duct, lingual nerve, and hypoglossal nerve. For pediatric patients weighing less than 10 kg, the procedure requires pediatric anesthetic expertise.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence rate below 5% in series of more than 50 cases; success rate greater than 95% in specialist centers (Baurmash 2003)

surgicalOptions.name: Marsupialization alone (intraoral, often under local anesthesia)
surgicalOptions.description: The cyst is de-roofed and its edges sutured open to maintain a drainage tract into the mouth. Technically simple and performable under local anesthesia, making it suitable for children and medically frail patients. Leaves the sublingual gland in situ.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Initial success 40-50%; recurrence 60-90% in long-term follow-up; now used as adjunct to gland excision or as temporizing measure only

surgicalOptions.name: Sublingual gland excision for plunging ranula (intraoral plus transcervical)
surgicalOptions.description: Combined approach: the cervical cyst fluid is drained through a small submandibular skin incision, and the sublingual gland is removed intraorally in the same operative session. Avoids need to excise the entire cervical pseudocyst, which has no epithelial lining and will collapse once the secretory source is removed.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence below 5% in published case series; 3-year cure rates exceed 95% at specialist centers

alternativeTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin A injection into the sublingual gland
alternativeTreatments.description: Off-label use to reduce sublingual gland secretion by blocking acetylcholine-mediated salivary stimulation. Case reports suggest temporary reduction in ranula size for 3-6 months. Not an established treatment but may be useful as a bridge to surgery in medically complex patients.

alternativeTreatments.name: Watchful waiting with dietary modification
alternativeTreatments.description: Reducing salivary stimuli (caffeine, citrus) and eating a soft diet reduces salivary output and may temporarily decrease ranula size in very small, minimally symptomatic lesions. Evidence is observational only; does not address the underlying ductal pathology.

whySeeSpecialist: Any floor-of-mouth swelling persisting beyond 4 weeks should be evaluated by an oral and maxillofacial surgeon to confirm the diagnosis and plan definitive treatment. Plunging ranulas must be assessed with MRI before surgery to define the cervical extent and avoid incomplete resection. Early specialist referral prevents diagnostic delay, unnecessary repeated aspirations, and failed marsupialization procedures.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose an oral and maxillofacial surgeon experienced in floor-of-mouth surgery and sublingual gland anatomy, who performs at least 10-20 ranula excisions per year. For plunging ranulas in neonates or infants, a center with combined pediatric anesthesia and maxillofacial surgical expertise is required. Centers with MRI and ultrasound-guided aspiration capability provide the most accurate preoperative planning.
hospitalCriteria: Oral and maxillofacial surgery with experience in sublingual gland excision under general anesthesia
MRI capability for preoperative plunging ranula mapping
Pediatric anesthesia for management of congenital or neonatal ranulas
Neonatal or pediatric intensive care unit availability for very large airway-compromising congenital ranulas
keyFacilities: Oral and maxillofacial surgery departments at children's hospitals or academic medical centers
Head and neck surgery centers with sublingual gland excision expertise
preventionStrategies: Avoid repetitive minor trauma to the floor of the mouth: address lower lip biting, tongue thrusting, and bruxism habits, particularly in children.
Seek prompt dental treatment for salivary gland stones (sialoliths) in Wharton's duct, which can obstruct sublingual duct drainage and predispose to ranula formation.
Inform dental and oral surgery providers about prior ranula history before any intraoral procedure so that floor-of-mouth trauma is minimized.
Monitor any floor-of-mouth swelling noticed after dental procedures — if persistent beyond 6 weeks, specialist review is warranted rather than watchful waiting.
lifestyleModifications: After surgical treatment, maintain a soft diet for 1-2 weeks to protect the surgical wound on the floor of the mouth.
Practice careful oral hygiene using gentle chlorhexidine rinses for 2 weeks post-surgery to prevent wound infection.
Avoid oral habits that traumatize the floor of the mouth (sucking hard sweets, lip biting) to reduce the small risk of contralateral ranula formation.
Attend all post-surgical follow-up appointments: the surgeon should inspect the floor of the mouth at 4-6 weeks post-excision and again at 3-6 months to confirm no recurrence.
Chew on the contralateral side during early recovery to reduce local trauma at the surgical site.

recommended: Soft, moist foods (porridge, yogurt, mashed potato, soup) for 2-4 weeks after surgery to prevent wound trauma
Adequate fluid intake to maintain salivary flow and prevent mucous inspissation in remaining salivary glands
Cool foods and drinks during the immediate post-operative period to reduce local swelling
Gradually return to normal diet as wound heals over 2-3 weeks
avoid: Hard, sharp, or crunchy foods for 2-3 weeks post-surgery
Very hot foods and drinks immediately after surgery
Carbonated beverages, which may irritate the surgical wound
Chewing gum, which increases jaw muscle activity and may displace the forming post-surgical scar

exerciseGuidelines: Normal physical activity can be resumed within 48-72 hours after uncomplicated intraoral sublingual gland excision. Heavy exercise and contact sports should be avoided for 2 weeks to prevent wound disruption. For transcervical incisions (plunging ranula surgery), strenuous neck movements and resistance training should be avoided for 4-6 weeks.
dailyManagement: Use chlorhexidine 0.12% rinse after every meal for 2 weeks following surgery, taking care to gently rinse rather than vigorous gargling.
Take prescribed analgesics on schedule for the first 48-72 hours post-surgery: ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily with food or paracetamol 1 g four times daily.
Monitor the surgical site daily: mild swelling and bruising are expected for 3-7 days; report increasing swelling, fever, or pus to the surgeon promptly.
Do not attempt to aspirate or manually deflate a recurrent ranula at home — this increases infection risk without treating the cause.
Attend follow-up at 4-6 weeks and 3-6 months post-surgery to confirm healing and identify early recurrence.
prognosis: Prognosis after definitive sublingual gland excision is excellent. Recurrence rates below 5% are consistently reported in specialist series of more than 50 cases, with long-term follow-up showing durable cure in 95-97% of patients. Functional outcomes are excellent: taste, salivary flow from the remaining salivary glands, and tongue sensation are preserved in the vast majority of cases when surgery is performed by experienced surgeons with careful nerve and duct identification. Lingual nerve numbness is the most common transient complication, reported in 5-10% of cases, with complete recovery in 90% within 3-6 months. Permanent lingual nerve injury is rare (less than 1%) in experienced hands. Plunging ranulas treated with combined intraoral gland excision and cervical drainage achieve similar cure rates. Marsupialization alone, without gland removal, achieves long-term cure in fewer than 30-40% of cases, with most patients experiencing one or more recurrences requiring further surgery. Large congenital ranulas treated emergently have a good prognosis for airway management but require careful long-term follow-up for recurrence.
recoveryTimeline: Intraoral swelling and discomfort peak in the first 48-72 hours post-surgery and resolve over 7-14 days. Soft diet is required for 1-2 weeks. Full oral function returns by 2-3 weeks. Lingual nerve sensitivity changes, if present, resolve in most patients over 6-12 weeks. Final assessment for cure is made at 3-6 months post-surgery.
complications: Lingual nerve paresthesia (numbness or tingling of the ipsilateral tongue) — the most common complication of sublingual gland excision; affects 5-10% of patients, resolves in 90% within 3-6 months.
Wharton's duct injury causing submandibular sialadenitis — rare but serious; prevented by careful duct identification and preservation during intraoral dissection.
Recurrence in 5% of cases even after complete sublingual gland excision, often representing contralateral gland involvement or incomplete removal.
Wound infection of the floor-of-mouth or transcervical incision in less than 2% of cases; managed with antibiotics and drainage.
Hypoglossal nerve injury (very rare, less than 0.5%) causing ipsilateral tongue weakness and deviation; avoided by careful surgical anatomy.
Airway compromise in large congenital or rapidly expanding plunging ranulas — a pre-operative risk requiring careful anesthetic planning and availability of difficult airway equipment.

supportResources.name: American Association of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeons — Patient Information
supportResources.description: Patient education resources covering oral soft-tissue conditions, surgical procedures, and recovery guidance.

supportResources.name: British Association of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeons — Ranula Patient Leaflet
supportResources.description: Patient information leaflets on oral and maxillofacial conditions including sublingual gland and salivary disorders.

supportResources.name: Oral Cancer Foundation — Salivary Gland Conditions
supportResources.description: Patient-oriented information on salivary gland pathology and when to seek specialist care.

confusedWithConditions.name: Salivary Gland Tumor
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Salivary gland tumors are solid or mixed-consistency masses, typically non-translucent, non-compressible, and firm on palpation. A ranula is soft, fluctuant, translucent, and bluish, and yields viscous mucous on aspiration. Imaging (MRI) and aspiration cytology definitively distinguish them; salivary gland neoplasms require surgical excision with oncologic margins.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dental Abscess
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dental abscesses are typically painful, erythematous, and associated with a carious or periodontally involved tooth. They resolve rapidly with antibiotics and dental treatment. Ranulas are painless, bluish, and unrelated to dental pathology; they persist and progressively enlarge until the sublingual gland is removed.

confusedWithConditions.name: Salivary Gland Stones
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Submandibular salivary gland stones (sialoliths) cause episodic painful swelling of the submandibular gland at mealtimes due to ductal obstruction. The swelling is submandibular glandular (palpable bimanually), not intraoral and translucent. Ultrasound or sialography shows the stone. Ranulas are floor-of-mouth translucent mucous cysts without calcification.

confusedWithConditions.name: Impacted Wisdom Teeth
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Impacted wisdom teeth cause pain, swelling, and trismus posteriorly in the mouth, typically associated with a specific visible or palpable tooth. Ranulas are anterior floor-of-mouth swellings with no dental association and no trismus unless they are very large. Dental X-ray and clinical examination distinguish them.

relatedConditions.name: Salivary Gland Tumor
relatedConditions.relevance: Salivary gland neoplasms are the most important differential diagnosis for ranulas; both arise in the salivary gland region of the floor of mouth and neck and are managed by the same maxillofacial surgery team.

relatedConditions.name: Salivary Gland Stones
relatedConditions.relevance: Ductal obstruction by sialoliths is a known precipitant of ranula formation; salivary stones and ranulas share the same anatomical space and are managed by the same specialist.

relatedConditions.name: Parotitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Inflammatory salivary gland diseases share the anatomy and clinical territory of ranulas; both require specialist oral and maxillofacial assessment and imaging.

relatedConditions.name: Oral Cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Floor-of-mouth swellings must always be evaluated with biopsy or imaging to exclude malignancy; oral cancer and ranulas can present similarly in elderly or immunosuppressed patients.

relatedConditions.name: Dentigerous Cyst
relatedConditions.relevance: Jaw cysts adjacent to impacted teeth may present as floor-of-mouth or neck swellings, overlapping with ranula differential diagnosis; radiographic and clinical examination distinguishes them.

faqs.question: What is a ranula?
faqs.answer: A ranula is a mucous cyst arising from the sublingual salivary gland, presenting as a soft, translucent, bluish swelling on the floor of the mouth. Simple ranulas stay in the mouth; plunging ranulas extend through the jaw muscle into the neck. The name comes from the Latin word for frog, describing the frog-belly appearance.

faqs.question: What causes a ranula?
faqs.answer: Ranulas are caused by blockage or rupture of the sublingual salivary gland ducts, allowing mucous saliva to pool in the floor-of-mouth tissue. Causes include ductal obstruction from stones, trauma from dental procedures, and chronic oral habits. In about 30-40% of cases no specific cause is identified.

faqs.question: Is a ranula dangerous?
faqs.answer: Simple oral ranulas are benign and not dangerous. Large plunging ranulas that extend into the neck can compress the airway or swallowing structures in rare cases. Congenital ranulas in newborns can cause breathing and feeding difficulty requiring urgent treatment. Most ranulas cause only cosmetic and functional nuisance.

faqs.question: How is a ranula treated?
faqs.answer: The definitive treatment is surgical excision of the ipsilateral sublingual gland, which removes the source of mucous secretion. This achieves cure in over 95% of cases. Marsupialization alone (cutting the cyst open) has a 60-90% recurrence rate and is only used as a temporary measure.

faqs.question: Will a ranula go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Spontaneous resolution occurs in roughly 20% of very small ranulas. Most ranulas persist, slowly enlarge, and intermittently deflate and refill. Definitive cure requires surgical removal of the sublingual gland. Observation alone is reasonable only for small, asymptomatic lesions in patients who decline surgery.

faqs.question: What is a plunging ranula?
faqs.answer: A plunging ranula is a mucous cyst that herniates from the floor of the mouth through a gap in the mylohyoid jaw muscle into the neck, producing a compressible neck mass. The intraoral component can be small or absent. MRI is needed to map its extent before surgery.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a ranula and a mucocele?
faqs.answer: Both are mucous extravasation cysts but differ in location. Mucoceles arise from the minor salivary glands of the lips and buccal mucosa; ranulas arise specifically from the sublingual gland on the floor of the mouth. Ranulas are larger, more clinically significant, and require surgical gland removal; mucoceles often resolve spontaneously or with simple excision.

faqs.question: How is a ranula diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is usually clinical: a soft, translucent bluish swelling on the floor of the mouth in a young patient. Aspiration yields viscous, high-amylase mucous fluid. MRI is required for plunging ranulas to map the cervical extent. Biopsy is rarely needed but performed when malignancy cannot be excluded.

faqs.question: What is the recurrence rate after ranula treatment?
faqs.answer: Marsupialization alone has a 60-90% recurrence rate because the secretory sublingual gland remains. Sublingual gland excision reduces recurrence to below 5% in specialist series. Sclerosant injection (OK-432) achieves complete resolution in 70-80% of selected plunging ranulas in pediatric series.

faqs.question: Does ranula surgery hurt?
faqs.answer: Sublingual gland excision is performed under general anesthesia and is painless during the procedure. Post-operative pain is mild to moderate and is managed with ibuprofen and paracetamol for 2-5 days. Floor-of-mouth swelling peaks at 48-72 hours and resolves over 7-14 days.

faqs.question: Can a ranula become cancerous?
faqs.answer: No. Ranulas are benign mucous pseudocysts with no malignant potential. They do not transform into cancer. However, any persistent floor-of-mouth swelling should be evaluated by an oral and maxillofacial surgeon to confirm the diagnosis and exclude salivary gland tumors, which can appear similarly.

faqs.question: Can ranulas occur in children?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ranulas most commonly affect children and young adults aged 10-30 years. Congenital ranulas are rare but important because they can cause airway compromise in newborns. Pediatric ranulas are treated with sublingual gland excision under general anesthesia.

faqs.question: How long does ranula surgery take?
faqs.answer: Intraoral sublingual gland excision typically takes 45-75 minutes under general anesthesia. Combined intraoral plus transcervical surgery for a plunging ranula takes 75-120 minutes. Most patients are discharged the same day or after one overnight stay.

faqs.question: Can a ranula affect speech or swallowing?
faqs.answer: Large ranulas can elevate the tongue and restrict tongue movement, causing mild slurring of lingual consonants. Very large lesions can cause dysphagia. These functional problems resolve completely after surgical removal in most patients.

faqs.question: Is aspiration a good treatment for a ranula?
faqs.answer: Simple aspiration of a ranula drains the fluid and provides temporary relief but does not treat the cause. The ranula refills within days to weeks in virtually all cases. Aspiration is useful for diagnostic confirmation but not as a definitive treatment. Sublingual gland excision is required for cure.

simpleName: Ranula (floor-of-mouth mucous cyst, sublingual gland cyst)
metaDescription: Ranula: a mucous cyst from the sublingual gland causing floor-of-mouth swelling. Learn about plunging ranulas, diagnosis by MRI and surgical treatment options.
